claim: Sustained transmission of Avian influenza A between humans is not observed.

result.claim: Sustained transmission of Avian influenza A between humans is not observed.
result.reasoning:
The claim that sustained transmission of Avian influenza A between humans is not observed is strongly supported by the evidence. Paper ID 41318623, an animal study, explicitly states that "Sustained human-to-human transmission of avian influenza A(H5N1) has not occurred." (prop_191188). This is further corroborated by paper ID 6051148cd9be808294c1cf858ef9168238a8a1e6, a systematic review with a high credibility rating (4.5/5), which notes "sporadic infections in humans" from A(H5Nx) clades and discusses the "on-going risk for zoonotic transmission and virus adaptation to mammals and humans," implying that sustained human-to-human transmission is not a current characteristic. Additionally, paper ID 83bd852f3ebb953269df313cf0d24b0edda684c5, an animal study, mentions that A(H9N2) viruses have "sporadically jumped species barriers to cause human infection" and highlights the need to monitor if these viruses are "acquiring properties that make them better suited to infect and spread among humans," further indicating that sustained human-to-human transmission is not yet established.

**Evidence Note:** 41318623, 6051148cd9be808294c1cf858ef9168238a8a1e6, 83bd852f3ebb953269df313cf0d24b0edda684c5

evidence.text: Mortality occurred in a patient with influenza A.
evidence.source: 36th International Symposium on Intensive Care and Emergency Medicine : Brussels, Belgium. 15-18 March 2016.

evidence.text: A(H9N2) influenza viruses are widespread in poultry in many parts of the world.
evidence.source: Genetically and Antigenically Divergent Influenza A(H9N2) Viruses Exhibit Differential Replication and Transmission Phenotypes in Mammalian Models

evidence.text: At least 119 countries conducted avian influenza virus surveillance in wild birds during 2008-2013.
evidence.source: Global avian influenza surveillance in wild birds: a strategy to capture viral diversity.

evidence.text: A(H9N2) influenza viruses are widespread in poultry in many parts of the world.
evidence.source: Genetically and Antigenically Divergent Influenza A(H9N2) Viruses Exhibit Differential Replication and Transmission Phenotypes in Mammalian Models

evidence.text: Mortality occurred in a patient with influenza A.

evidence.text: The risk of transmission is negligible compared to when the virus first left the host, when the virus is detected in a matter of hours, tens of hours, or days.
evidence.source: Transmission risk of viruses in large mucosalivary droplets on the surface of objects: A time-based analysis.

evidence.text: Lassa fever virus transmission can occur through contact with human food or water with excreta (urine or faeces) of the animal reservoir.
evidence.source: The Dilemma in the Management of an Asymptomatic Patient with Confirmed Lassa Fever Viremia(a Case Report): Implication for Policy Action in the Control of Infectious Diseases of Public Health Importance in Nigeria

evidence.text: SARS-CoV-2 is mainly transmitted by respiratory droplets produced by an infected person.
evidence.source: Transmission risk of viruses in large mucosalivary droplets on the surface of objects: A time-based analysis.

evidence.text: There has been evidence of human-to-human transmission of Nipah virus (NiV).
evidence.source: Loss in lung volume and changes in the immune response demonstrate disease progression in African green monkeys infected by small-particle aerosol and intratracheal exposure to Nipah virus.

evidence.text: A(H9N2) viruses have sporadically jumped species barriers to cause human infection for over 20 years.
evidence.source: Genetically and Antigenically Divergent Influenza A(H9N2) Viruses Exhibit Differential Replication and Transmission Phenotypes in Mammalian Models

evidence.text: H. influenzae was isolated and detected from the patients’ respiratory tracts in this study.
evidence.source: Distribution characteristics of aerosol microorganisms in bronchoscopy room and the risk assessment of nosocomial infection.

evidence.text: The influenza virus gene pool in aquatic birds is perpetuated by transmission throughout the year.
evidence.source: Evolution and ecology of influenza A viruses

evidence.text: NiV can be transmitted to humans through inhalation, contact, or consumption of NiV contaminated foods.
evidence.source: Loss in lung volume and changes in the immune response demonstrate disease progression in African green monkeys infected by small-particle aerosol and intratracheal exposure to Nipah virus.

evidence.text: Avian influenza viruses (AIV) circulate in wild and domestic bird populations.
evidence.source: A Rapid Review Contrasting the Evidence on Avian Influenza A(H5Nx) Clades 2.3.4.4b and 2.3.2.1c in Humans

evidence.text: Sustained human-to-human transmission of avian influenza A(H5N1) has not occurred.
evidence.source: Influenza mRNA vaccine reduces pathogenicity and transmission of A(H5N1) virus in a ferret model.

evidence.text: The human receptor for influenza A virus (IAV) was detected by Sambucus Nigra Lectin (SNA) in the nasal mucosa, trachea, bronchi, bronchioles and alveoli of the porcine respiratory tract.
evidence.source: The avian influenza A virus receptor SA-α2,3-Gal is expressed in the porcine nasal mucosa sustaining the pig as a mixing vessel for new influenza viruses.

evidence.text: The poultry surveillance system in Vietnam was established to identify influenza viruses present in live bird markets.
evidence.source: Genetically and Antigenically Divergent Influenza A(H9N2) Viruses Exhibit Differential Replication and Transmission Phenotypes in Mammalian Models

evidence.text: Influenza viruses currently circulating in humans and pigs in North America originated from the avian reservoir before the 1918 Spanish influenza pandemic.
evidence.source: Evolution and ecology of influenza A viruses

evidence.text: The global spread of highly pathogenic avian influenza A(H5N1) viruses poses a serious pandemic threat.
evidence.source: Influenza mRNA vaccine reduces pathogenicity and transmission of A(H5N1) virus in a ferret model.

evidence.text: A(H9N2) viruses have sporadically jumped species barriers to cause human infection for over 20 years.
